sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, a 30-year-old male, presents with a 1-month history of worsening lower back pain radiating to his left leg, which worsens with standing and improves with lying down.
He has a 30-year history of intermittent back pain, with a notable exacerbation following a fall from a roof 30 years ago, which was evaluated with an MRI showing a possible fracture.
He describes the pain as an electrical toothache, rating it 6/10 currently, with a maximum of 13/10.
He has tried NSAIDs and physical therapy without relief.
Past Medical History: The patient has a history of a possible fracture from a fall from a roof 30 years ago, with an MRI showing a possible fracture, but no further information is available.
Review of Systems: The patient reports numbness and tingling in his lower extremities, with weakness in both legs.
Coughing and sneezing exacerbate the pain.
Current Medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital Signs: Not mentioned.
Physical Exam: On examination, the patient has pain to palpation at the L4 vertebrae, with pain on flexion and extension of the back.
He has 4/5 strength in the left leg and 5/5 strength in the right leg.
Reflexes are brisk and motor and sensory are intact in both lower extremities.
Test Results: MRI shows a disk herniation at the level of L4-L5 vertebrae with associated nerve root impingement.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has acute disk herniation at L4-L5 with nerve root impingement, causing lower back pain and leg pain.
Problem list: Acute disk herniation at L4-L5 with nerve root impingement; lower back pain; leg pain.
Plan:
I prescribed an epidural steroid injection to reduce inflammation at the site of the herniation.
I will refer the patient to physical therapy to strengthen the area.
I want the patient to follow up with me in about a month to assess the efficacy of the injection.
Follow-up: I want the patient to see me again in about a month to assess the efficacy of the epidural steroid injection.
